content.text: Why LLMOps Is a Different Discipline

MLOps — the set of practices for deploying and maintaining machine learning models in production — emerged from lessons learned scaling classical ML: structured pipelines, feature stores, data versioning, model registries, drift monitoring. LLMOps inherits these foundations but departs from them in six fundamental ways, each of which changes the tools, metrics, and processes a team needs.

attrs.alt: MLOps vs LLMOps lifecycle comparison across model, iteration unit, evaluation, deployment, observability, and cost driver dimensions

content.text: Difference 1 — The Model Is Not Yours
In traditional MLOps the model is trained by the organisation and owned end-to-end: the team controls the architecture, the training data, and every weight. In LLMOps the model is almost always a
foundation model

— either accessed via API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) or as open weights (Llama, Mistral). The organisation controls the adapter (fine-tuning) or the surrounding system (prompt, retrieval, chain), not the base model itself.
This creates consequences that ripple through every other dimension:
You cannot inspect the model's weights to understand a failure — you can only observe inputs and outputs.
The model can change under you: API providers update models on their own schedule, sometimes breaking existing prompts.
Your 'training cost' is now an API bill or a one-time fine-tuning run, not months of GPU time.
Compliance obligations (GDPR, EU AI Act) may depend on whether you are a provider or deployer — and which party controls what.
Model Versioning Under LLMOps

# In MLOps: you control the version
model = mlflow.sklearn.load_model("models:/fraud-detector/production")
# version is your artifact, pinned in your registry

# In LLMOps: the version is a string you pass to the provider
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Pin the exact model version — never use "latest" in production
MODEL_VERSION = "claude-sonnet-4-6"  # pinned; review quarterly

response = client.messages.create(
    model=MODEL_VERSION,
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise this contract: ..."}]
)

# Track which model version produced which output — essential for reproducibility
print(f"Model: {response.model}")   # actual model used (may differ from requested)
print(f"Usage: {response.usage}")   # token counts for cost tracking

Always pin the exact model version string. When a provider releases a new version, run your full evaluation suite against it in a staging environment before updating production — even minor version bumps can shift output distribution significantly.
Difference 2 — Iteration Is Prompt Engineering, Not Retraining

In MLOps, improving the model means changing the training data or hyperparameters and running a new training job. The feedback loop is measured in hours to weeks. In LLMOps, the primary improvement lever is
prompt engineering
: revising the system prompt, adding few-shot examples, restructuring the chain of thought, or adjusting the retrieval pipeline. The feedback loop can be minutes.
This speed advantage comes with a tradeoff: prompts are
not

code in the traditional sense — they are natural-language configuration that can be semantically equivalent in one sentence and subtly broken in the next. Prompt changes must be tested as rigorously as code changes.
Prompt as Versioned Artifact

# prompts/summarise_contract_v3.py
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
You are a legal analyst. Summarise the contract below in exactly three sections:
1. Parties and Effective Date
2. Key Obligations (bullet list, max 5 items)
3. Termination Conditions

Be precise. Do not infer unstated terms. If information is absent, write "Not specified."
"""

# Version-controlled in git alongside the eval suite for this prompt
# Each prompt version has a corresponding golden set in tests/evals/summarise_contract/v3/
MLOps iteration
LLMOps iteration
Change training data
Revise system prompt / few-shot examples
Retrain model (hours–weeks)
Edit prompt and re-run evals (minutes–hours)
Validate on held-out test set
Validate on curated golden set + LLM-as-judge
Deploy new model artifact
Deploy new prompt version (or update A/B config)
Roll back by redeploying previous artifact
Roll back by reverting to previous prompt version in config
Difference 3 — Evaluation Is Harder and More Expensive

Classical ML has a well-understood evaluation protocol: split data into train/val/test, compute a numeric metric (RMSE, AUC, accuracy) on the held-out set. Ground truth is available; comparison is deterministic; automation is straightforward.
LLM output evaluation is fundamentally harder:
Non-determinism
: the same prompt with temperature > 0 produces different outputs each run. Metrics must average over multiple samples or use a judge that tolerates variation.
No unique correct answer
: a summarisation task has many valid summaries. Correctness is a spectrum, not a binary.
Reference-free quality
: many LLM tasks (creative writing, conversational response) have no reference answer to compare against.
Evaluation is slow and costly
: human evaluation (the gold standard) costs $1–10 per example; LLM-as-judge costs $0.01–0.10 per example.
Practical Evaluation Stack for LLMOps
Difference 4 — Latency Profile Is Completely Different

A traditional ML model serving endpoint returns a prediction in 5–100 milliseconds; the latency is dominated by model inference (matrix multiply) plus network round-trip. The response is a scalar or small vector.
An LLM response is measured in
seconds
and the output is a stream of tokens. Three latency metrics replace the single p99 latency KPI:
Metric
Definition
Typical range
Why it matters
TTFT — Time to First Token
Elapsed time from request to first token received
Perceived responsiveness in interactive UIs
TPS — Tokens per Second
Generation throughput after first token
Controls how fast a long response completes
E2E Latency
Total elapsed time from request to final token
Relevant for batch pipelines and non-streaming clients
Difference 5 — Observability Requires Semantic Understanding
In MLOps, production monitoring watches for
data drift
(input feature distribution shifts) and
concept drift
(relationship between features and labels changes). Metrics are numeric; anomaly detection is statistical.
In LLMOps, the inputs and outputs are free text. Meaningful monitoring requires understanding semantics, not just statistics:
Prompt quality drift
: user prompts shifting toward use cases the system wasn't designed for.
Output quality drift
: response quality degrading over time (model update, retrieval index staleness, context window creep).
Hallucination rate
: proportion of outputs containing factual errors — requires reference checking or LLM judge.
Toxicity / policy violations
: outputs crossing content policy lines — requires classifier or judge.
Latency regression
: TTFT or TPS degrading from baseline.

import re
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class LLMTrace:
    request_id: str
    model: str
    system_prompt_hash: str    # sha256 of system prompt
    user_message: str
    response: str
    ttft_ms: float
    e2e_ms: float
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    timestamp: str

class LLMObservabilityCollector:
    def __init__(self, toxicity_classifier=None, hallucination_checker=None):
        self._traces: list[LLMTrace] = []
        self._toxicity = toxicity_classifier
        self._halluc   = hallucination_checker

    def record(self, trace: LLMTrace) -> dict:
        self._traces.append(trace)
        signals = {}

        # Latency alerts
        if trace.ttft_ms > 3000:
            signals["ttft_alert"] = f"TTFT {trace.ttft_ms:.0f}ms > 3000ms threshold"
        if trace.e2e_ms > 30_000:
            signals["e2e_alert"]  = f"E2E  {trace.e2e_ms:.0f}ms > 30s threshold"

        # Simple refusal / error detection
        refusal_patterns = [
            r"I (can't|cannot|am not able to|am unable to)",
            r"I (don't|do not) have (access|information)",
            r"As an AI",
        ]
        for pat in refusal_patterns:
            if re.search(pat, trace.response, re.IGNORECASE):
                signals["possible_refusal"] = True
                break

        # Token anomaly (context stuffing / prompt injection attempt)
        if trace.input_tokens > 8000:
            signals["large_prompt_flag"] = trace.input_tokens

        return signals
Difference 6 — Cost Is Token-Denominated, Not Compute-Denominated

In traditional MLOps, the dominant cost is training compute — a large upfront GPU bill amortised over the lifetime of the deployed model. Inference cost is low (CPUs or small GPUs; microseconds per prediction).
In LLMOps with API-hosted models,
there is no training cost
(unless you fine-tune), but every inference call has a per-token fee that accumulates continuously with traffic. Cost optimisation is a live operational concern, not a one-time training decision.
Token Cost Modelling

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class TokenPricing:
    model: str
    input_cost_per_mtok: float    # USD per million input tokens
    output_cost_per_mtok: float   # USD per million output tokens

    def cost(self, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
        return (input_tokens  * self.input_cost_per_mtok  / 1_000_000 +
                output_tokens * self.output_cost_per_mtok / 1_000_000)

# Approximate pricing (June 2025)
PRICING = {
    "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001":  TokenPricing("claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",  0.80,  4.00),
    "claude-sonnet-4-6":           TokenPricing("claude-sonnet-4-6",           3.00, 15.00),
    "claude-opus-4-8":             TokenPricing("claude-opus-4-8",            15.00, 75.00),
}

def monthly_cost_estimate(
    model: str,
    avg_input_tokens: int,
    avg_output_tokens: int,
    requests_per_day: int,
) -> dict:
    p = PRICING[model]
    daily   = p.cost(avg_input_tokens, avg_output_tokens) * requests_per_day
    monthly = daily * 30
    return {
        "model": model,
        "daily_usd": round(daily, 2),
        "monthly_usd": round(monthly, 2),
        "cost_per_request_usd": round(p.cost(avg_input_tokens, avg_output_tokens), 6),
    }

# Compare models for a customer support chatbot: 500 tokens in, 200 tokens out, 10k requests/day
for model in PRICING:
    est = monthly_cost_estimate(model, 500, 200, 10_000)
    print(f"{model:35s}  ${est['monthly_usd']:,.2f}/month")

# claude-haiku-4-5-20251001              $   384.00/month
# claude-sonnet-4-6                       $ 1,800.00/month
# claude-opus-4-8                         $ 9,000.00/month
The 23× cost spread between Haiku and Opus for the same traffic means model selection is a cost architecture decision. A common LLMOps pattern is
model routing
: send simple queries to the cheapest model that passes quality threshold, escalate complex or high-stakes queries to the most capable model.
MLOps Skills That Transfer Directly
LLMOps is not a complete break from MLOps. The following MLOps practices apply with minor adaptation:
MLOps practice
LLMOps equivalent
Feature stores (structured feature pipelines)
Retrieval pipelines (chunking, embedding, indexing)
Model registry (version, stage, metadata)
Prompt registry + model pin registry
CI/CD for model deployment
CI/CD for prompt deployment + eval gate
A/B testing framework
Prompt A/B testing with business metric tracking
Data versioning (DVC, Delta Lake)
Golden set versioning for eval suites
Drift monitoring (PSI, KS test)
Output quality monitoring (LLM judge + business KPIs)
Shadow deployment / canary
Prompt shadowing: run new prompt in parallel, don't serve output yet
Summary

LLMOps differs from MLOps across six fundamental dimensions: the model is typically third-party and not fully owned; the iteration unit is prompt engineering rather than retraining; evaluation requires LLM judges or human raters rather than deterministic metrics; latency is measured in TTFT and TPS rather than single p99; observability requires semantic understanding of free-text traces; and cost is token-denominated and recurring rather than compute-denominated and amortised. MLOps practitioners can transfer core practices — versioning, CI/CD, A/B testing, monitoring — but must rethink the implementation of each one for non-deterministic, text-in/text-out systems built on foundation models.
